sentence: HPI: The patient, Maria Martin, a 76-year-old female, presents for follow-up.
She reports being "pretty good" despite having poorly controlled sugars, as evidenced by an A1c of 9.9.
She has not increased her glimepiride dose as previously instructed, and her current dose is 2 mg twice a day.
She has been trying to eat healthier, but not successfully, as she still consumes wheat bread and occasionally has peanut butter sandwiches.
She has not made any significant changes to her diet.
She reports being up to date on her eye exams, having recently obtained new glasses and undergoing cataract surgery.
Her high blood pressure has been stable, with a recent reading of 116/ unknown.
She is currently on a combination of medications, including metoprolol, lisinopril with hydrochlorothiazide, and amlodipine.
She reports tolerating atorvastatin, but experiencing peripheral neuropathy in her feet and legs, as well as pain in her hips and upper legs.
She is a smoker and has not attempted to quit.
She denies headaches, dizziness, shortness of breath, or chest pains.
She reports occasional difficulty urinating and occasional nocturia.
Denies shortness of breath or coughing
Denies chest pains or pressure or tightness
Glimepiride 2 mg twice a day for diabetes
Metoprolol 50 mg once a day for hypertension
Lisinopril with hydrochlorothiazide 20/25 mg once a day for hypertension
Amlodipine 10 mg once a day for hypertension
Aspirin (not mentioned in the transcript, but commonly used for vascular disease)
Heart exam: regular rhythm, normal rate, S1 and S2 are present, no murmurs
Pulses are palpable on the dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial
Mildly dry skin, no skin breakdown
Mildly decreased sensation in the tips of the first and second toes
Assessment: The patient has poorly controlled diabetes, hypertension, and hyperlipidemia, with evidence of peripheral neuropathy and vascular disease in the legs.
Problem list: Diabetes mellitus type 2; hypertension; hyperlipidemia; peripheral neuropathy; vascular disease in the legs; cataracts.
I increased the patient's glimepiride dose to 4 mg twice a day and instructed her to take two of the 2 mg pills until she receives the new prescription.
I also instructed her to continue metformin.
I advised her to watch for signs of low blood sugar, including weakness, shakiness, and sweating.
I wrote a new prescription for 4 mg glimepiride once a day, replacing the previous 2 mg dose.
I continued the patient on her current medications, including metoprolol, lisinopril with hydrochlorothiazide, and amlodipine.
I also instructed her to continue atorvastatin.
I will recheck her blood work, including kidney function, and see her back in 4 months.
The patient will be seen in 4 months to recheck her blood work and medications.
